top_signals.reason: 4 politicians bought MSFT: Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, Mcguire
3 politicians bought JPM: Fetterman, Keating, Khanna

decisions.reasoning: Strongest signal in the set: 4 independent politicians across both chambers (Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, McGuire) purchased within a ~6-week window. Bipartisan buying reduces the 'shared news event' concern somewhat. Amounts are small ($1K-$15K), suggesting personal portfolio allocation rather than a high-conviction insider play, but the multi-buyer consensus is the best we have here. Warner sits on Senate Intelligence/Banking — potential committee relevance to cloud/AI contracts. MSFT also has genuine sector tailwinds (Azure, AI/Copilot). Score of 100 is a model artifact; real conviction is moderate.
decisions.key_risk: Trades are minimum-bracket ($1K-$15K), suggesting routine diversification rather than a strong insider signal. No watchdog alerts, zero news sentiment, and NORMAL velocity all reduce conviction. The cluster may simply reflect MSFT's status as a default blue-chip buy, not privileged information.

decisions.reasoning: 3 politicians (Fetterman, Keating, Khanna) bought within a tight 7-day window (May 13–19), which is the most temporally clustered signal in the dataset — a mild positive. Khanna's position is slightly larger ($15K-$50K), adding marginal weight. Banking sector has been volatile around Fed rate decisions and tariff/macro headlines, which could explain a shared reaction rather than privileged info. Keating (House Foreign Affairs) and Khanna (House Armed Services/Commerce) have limited direct committee overlap with banking regulation, reducing the conflict-of-interest premium.
decisions.key_risk: Tight clustering may reflect a shared macro/news trigger (e.g., Fed commentary, bank earnings) rather than independent conviction. No watchdog alerts or elevated sentiment. Small trade sizes dominate. Banking faces macro headwinds (credit risk, rate uncertainty). Conviction is genuine but not high.

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 buyers (Boozman and Fetterman), and the dates are spread apart (Apr 3 vs May 11) — reducing clustering conviction. Fetterman appears across MSFT, JPM, and GOOGL simultaneously, suggesting broad portfolio rebalancing rather than stock-specific insight. No watchdog alerts, no news sentiment, no velocity spike. The signal does not rise above 'two politicians bought a mega-cap tech stock.'
decisions.key_risk: Fetterman buying GOOGL, MSFT, and JPM on the same date (Apr 3) is a clear portfolio sweep, not targeted insider activity. Committee relevance is weak. GOOGL faces ongoing antitrust risk. Insufficient signal differentiation to justify a position.

decisions.reasoning: CEG (Constellation Energy) is the most interesting non-obvious pick here. Two Senate buyers (Boozman, Warner) within 7 days. Warner on Senate Intelligence and Banking has potential relevance to energy infrastructure/grid security policy. CEG is a nuclear energy play with genuine tailwinds from AI data center power demand and clean energy legislation. The signal is thin (2 buyers, small amounts), but the sector specificity makes this less likely a default blue-chip buy. HOLD pending stronger confirmation — worth watching for additional buyers or watchdog flags.
decisions.key_risk: Only 2 buyers at minimum bracket sizes. No watchdog alerts. Nuclear/energy stocks are highly policy-sensitive — regulatory or permitting setbacks could rapidly reverse any political tailwind. Cluster could reflect shared reading of the same energy policy news rather than privileged access.

decisions.reasoning: Warner and McGuire buying AAPL at the same time they bought MSFT, CEG, and other tickers strongly suggests a broad portfolio rebalancing event, not targeted conviction in AAPL specifically. AAPL faces real near-term headwinds: tariff exposure on China manufacturing, iPhone demand softness, and App Store regulatory risk. Two politicians buying the world's largest company is essentially noise. No watchdog alerts, no sentiment signal, no velocity.
decisions.key_risk: Signal is almost certainly portfolio diversification, not insider activity. AAPL's China tariff exposure and lack of committee-specific relevance for either buyer makes this a low-quality signal. Buying into a known headwind without stronger confirmation is unjustified.
